yaml.title: Module Overview
yaml.subtitle: From Zero to Understanding — Module Overview

markdown.headingText: What is Time Series Analysis? The Big Picture
markdown.markdown: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > 📌 **How to use this guide:** Read each section slowly. The analogies > are there to help you build intuition *before* touching the maths. > Every section ends with a self-check question — answer it in your own > words before moving on. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The core idea in one sentence **Time series analysis is the art of listening to data over time, understanding its patterns, and using those patterns to predict the future.** ## A real-world analogy to make it stick Imagine you work at a coffee shop and every day you record how many coffees you sell. After a year, you notice: - Sales have been **growing overall** (more customers each month) → this is **Trend** - Sales **spike every Monday morning** and dip on Sundays → this is **Seasonality** - One week sales dropped because of a snowstorm → this is **Random noise** Time series analysis is *exactly* this: you look at your coffee sales data, pull apart these different layers, understand what drives each one, and then use that understanding to say "next month we'll probably sell around X coffees." ## Why does it matter beyond this module? This thinking transfers to **any subject or job** that involves data collected over time: | Field | Time Series application | |-----------------|-----------------------------------------| | Finance | Predicting stock prices, exchange rates | | Healthcare | Tracking disease spread over weeks | | Marketing | Forecasting monthly sales | | Engineering | Monitoring machine performance | | Economics | Modelling GDP, inflation, unemployment | | Climate Science | Analysing temperature changes | The concepts you learn here — patterns, prediction, uncertainty — are universal. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** In your own words, what is a time series? Give one > example from your own life. > Time series is the way we can analyse data overtime, find its patterns to predict what is going to happen in the future. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # The Four Components of Any Time Series ## The recipe analogy Think of a time series like a recipe. A cake is made of flour, sugar, butter, and eggs — but you only see the finished cake. Time series analysis is like *reverse-engineering* a recipe: you have the finished cake (your data), and you want to figure out how much of each ingredient went in. The "ingredients" of any time series are: ### Trend (T) — The long-term direction **What it is:** The overall movement of your data upwards or downwards over a long period. **Example:** Global average temperatures have been rising for 100 years. If you plot this, the long-term *direction* is upward — that upward slope is the trend. **Key insight:** A trend doesn't have to be a straight line. It can curve, slow down, or reverse. ### Seasonality (S) — The repeating pattern **What it is:** A regular, predictable pattern that repeats within a fixed period (usually within one year). **Example:** Ice cream sales are always high in summer and low in winter — every single year, without fail. This is seasonal behaviour. **Key insight:** Seasonality is *predictable and regular*. It completes itself within a calendar year. ### Cyclical Component (C) — The long, irregular wave **What it is:** Patterns that repeat over multiple years, but are not as regular as seasons. Usually linked to economic cycles. **Example:** After the 2009 financial crisis, nearly every economic indicator dropped. This is a cyclical downturn — it lasts years, not months, and cannot be predicted with the same precision as seasons. **Key difference from Seasonality:** Seasonality repeats within a year. Cyclical patterns take *more than 2 years* to complete and are not perfectly regular. ### Random / Residual (R) — The unexplained noise **What it is:** Everything left over after you remove trend, seasonality, and cyclical patterns. This is the unexplained, random variation. **Example:** One particular Tuesday, your coffee shop sold 300 coffees instead of the usual 200. Maybe a conference was in town. You couldn't have predicted it — that extra 100 is "random." **Key insight:** If your model is good, the residuals should look like pure random noise (no pattern left). ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The Decomposition Equation Once you accept that data is made of these components, you can write a mathematical expression for any time series observation $y_t$ at time $t$: ### Additive Model Use this when the seasonal swings stay roughly **the same size** over time. $$y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t$$ **When to use:** The ups and downs in your data look the same height throughout the series (constant variance). ### Multiplicative Model Use this when the seasonal swings get **bigger as the trend grows**. $$y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t$$ **When to use:** The wiggling in your data gets wider over time (increasing variance). **Tip:** You can convert a multiplicative model into an additive one by taking the logarithm of your data: $$\log(y_t) = \log(T_t) + \log(S_t) + \log(R_t)$$ ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You're looking at monthly electricity usage data. > In summer and winter the spikes get bigger every year. Should you use > an additive or multiplicative model? Why? > > You should use the multiplicative model because the spikes get bigger every year. Different than the additive model that stays the same size. > ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Classical Decomposition in R ## What decomposition does Decomposition is the process of separating a time series into its components so you can study each one individually. Think of it like using a prism to split white light into individual colours. In R, the main tools are: ``` r # Load the forecast library (your main toolbox for this module) library(forecast) # Basic decomposition plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "additive")) plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "multiplicative")) # Better decomposition: STL (Seasonal & Trend using Loess) stl_result <- stl(my_timeseries, s.window = "periodic") autoplot(stl_result) ``` ## STL Decomposition — the preferred method STL stands for **Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess** (Loess is a type of local regression). **Why STL is better than basic decomposition:** - It handles data at both ends of the series (no missing values at the start or end) - It is **robust** — extreme values (outliers) don't distort the components - You can control how flexible the trend and seasonal components are **The trade-off:** STL only works for **additive** models. For multiplicative data, take logs first, apply STL, then back-transform. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** What is the difference between `decompose()` and > `stl()` in R? Which should you prefer and why? > > For decompose() you miss values at the start or the end, outliers distort the components. stl() only works for additive models. > ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Classical Forecasting Models — Exponential Smoothing ## The core idea: recent data matters more Exponential smoothing is based on a simple but powerful idea: **the most recent observations should have more influence on your forecast than older ones.** Imagine you're estimating how long your commute will take tomorrow. Yesterday's commute matters more than the commute from 6 months ago. But you don't completely ignore the older data — you just weight it less. That's exactly what exponential smoothing does: it applies *exponentially decreasing weights* to past observations. ## The three exponential smoothing methods Think of these as tools with increasing capability: ### 1. Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) **Use when:** Data has **no trend** and **no seasonality** — just a level that fluctuates randomly. **The key parameter — α (alpha):** | Alpha value | Meaning | Practical effect | |--------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------| | α close to 1 | Short memory | Only the very last observation matters | | α close to 0 | Long memory | All past observations weighted equally | | α = 0.83 (example) | Short memory | Forecast closely tracks last value | **The formula:** $$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$$ This says: "My next forecast = (alpha × what just happened) + (1-alpha × what I previously predicted)" **In R:** ``` r library(forecast) # Method 1: specific SES function ses_model <- ses(my_timeseries, h = 5) # h = number of periods to forecast # Method 2: using the ETS family ses_model <- ets(my_timeseries, model = "ANN") # ANN = Error(Additive), No trend, No seasonality ``` **What the forecast looks like:** A flat horizontal line — because with no trend or seasonality, the best guess for all future periods is the same value. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ### 2. Holt's Method (Double Exponential Smoothing) **Use when:** Data has a **trend** but **no seasonality**. **Two parameters:** - **α (alpha):** Controls the level (same as SES) - **β (beta):** Controls how quickly the trend estimate updates **In R:** ``` r holt_model <- holt(my_timeseries, h = 5) # Damped trend (trend slows down over time — often more realistic) holt_damped <- holt(my_timeseries, damped = TRUE, h = 5) ``` **What the forecast looks like:** A sloped line following the trend direction. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ### 3. Holt-Winters Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing) **Use when:** Data has both **trend** and **seasonality** — the most complete model. **Three parameters:** - **α (alpha):** Level - **β (beta):** Trend - **γ (gamma):** Seasonality **Two versions:** - **Additive Holt-Winters:** Seasonal effect is constant in size - **Multiplicative Holt-Winters:** Seasonal effect grows with the level **In R:** ``` r # Additive hw_add <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "additive", h = 12) # Multiplicative hw_mult <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12) # Let R choose automatically using ETS auto_model <- ets(my_timeseries) summary(auto_model) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The ETS Framework — the big family All exponential smoothing methods belong to the **ETS** family: **ETS = Error · Trend · Seasonality** Each letter takes a value: - **Error:** A (additive) or M (multiplicative) - **Trend:** N (none), A (additive), Ad (additive damped), M (multiplicative) - **Seasonality:** N (none), A (additive), M (multiplicative) | Model name | ETS code | Use case | |------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | Simple Exp. Smoothing | ETS(A,N,N) | No trend, no seasonality | | Holt's Method | ETS(A,A,N) | Trend, no seasonality | | Holt-Winters Additive | ETS(A,A,A) | Trend + additive seasonality | | Holt-Winters Multiplicative | ETS(A,A,M) | Trend + multiplicative seasonality | ``` r # Let R automatically find the best ETS model best_model <- ets(my_timeseries) summary(best_model) forecast(best_model, h = 12) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You have monthly data showing sales with an upward > trend and seasonal peaks at Christmas. Which ETS method would you > choose? What would α, β, and γ control? > `I would choose Holt-Winters Multiplicative because you can see an increase in sales trand, and seasonal peaks every Christmas. The Beta would be the Trend, Gamma seasonality and I am not sure about alpha.` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # How to Measure Forecast Accuracy ## Why this matters Any model can give you *a* forecast. But is it a *good* forecast? You need numbers to measure how far off your predictions are from reality. The standard approach: 1. **Split your data** into a training set (to build the model) and a test set (to check accuracy) 2. **Forecast** the test period using your model 3. **Compare** forecasts to actual values using error measures ## The main error measures Let $y_t$ = actual value and $\hat{y}_t$ = forecasted value. The error is $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$. | Measure | Formula | What it tells you | |------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum |e_t|$ | Average size of errors, ignoring direction | | **RMSE** (Root Mean Square Error) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2}$ | Penalises large errors more heavily | | **MAPE** (Mean Absolute % Error) | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \left|\frac{e_t}{y_t}\right|$ | Error as a percentage (good for comparison) | | **AIC** (Akaike Information Criterion) | — | Penalises model complexity; **lower = better** | | **BIC** (Bayesian Information Criterion) | — | Similar to AIC, stronger complexity penalty | **The golden rule for model selection:** When comparing two models, the one with the **lower AIC/BIC and lower RMSE/MAE** is preferred. ``` r # Get accuracy metrics in R accuracy(forecast_result, test_data) # Compare AIC between two models summary(model1)$aic summary(model2)$aic ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You compare two models. Model A has AIC = 1200 and > RMSE = 45. Model B has AIC = 1180 and RMSE = 52. Which would you > choose and why? (Hint: there's no single right answer — discuss the > trade-off.) ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # ARIMA Models — The Statistical Engine ## The shift in thinking Everything up to now (exponential smoothing) was about finding patterns and extrapolating them. ARIMA is different — it's based on **statistical theory** about how data relates to its own past values. The key concept: **autocorrelation** — the idea that today's value is correlated with yesterday's value, last week's value, etc. Think of it like your mood. If you're happy today, you're probably somewhat happy tomorrow. Your mood is correlated with your own past mood. Data often behaves the same way. ## Stationarity — the crucial requirement Before fitting an ARIMA model, your data must be **stationary**. This is perhaps the most important concept in the second half of the module. ### What does stationary mean? A time series is stationary if it has **three properties that don't change over time:** 1. **Constant mean** — the average doesn't drift up or down 2. **Constant variance** — the spread of the data stays roughly the same 3. **Constant covariance** — if observation at time 1 is correlated with observation at time 3 (lag 2), then this same correlation exists throughout the entire series **The visual test:** If you cover different parts of your time series plot with your hand and each section looks basically the same — same level, same spread — it's likely stationary. ### Why does stationarity matter? ARIMA models are built on statistical equations that only work when the data's statistical properties are stable. If the mean is drifting, the model can't figure out what "normal" is. ### How to make data stationary **Problem: non-constant mean (trend)** → Solution: **Differencing** Differencing means computing the change from one period to the next: $$\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$$ If differencing once doesn't work, you can do it twice (second-order differencing). The number of times you difference is the **d** in ARIMA(p, d, q). **Problem: non-constant variance** → Solution: **Log transformation** Taking the log of your data compresses large values and stabilises variance. This is the same reason multiplicative seasonal data responds to log transformation. ``` r # Visual check: does the data look stationary? plot(my_timeseries) # Differencing in R diff_series <- diff(my_timeseries) # First difference diff2_series <- diff(my_timeseries, differences = 2) # Second difference # Formal statistical test for stationarity (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) library(tseries) adf.test(my_timeseries) # p-value < 0.05 → stationary # p-value > 0.05 → NOT stationary, need differencing ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The ARIMA building blocks **ARIMA(p, d, q)** is made of three parts: ### AR(p) — AutoRegressive **The idea:** Today's value depends on its own past values. $$y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \ldots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$ **Analogy:** "My commute today (y_t) is partly explained by my commute yesterday (y\_{t-1}) and the day before (y\_{t-2})." The **p** tells you how many past values to include. ### MA(q) — Moving Average **The idea:** Today's value depends on past *forecast errors* (not past values themselves). $$y_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \ldots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$ **Analogy:** "When I was late yesterday, I leave earlier today to compensate — I'm reacting to past *errors* in my schedule." The **q** tells you how many past errors to include. ### I(d) — Integrated **The idea:** The d stands for how many times you need to *difference* the data to make it stationary. - d = 0: already stationary - d = 1: one differencing needed (most common) - d = 2: two differences needed (rare) ### Putting it together: ARIMA(p, d, q) ``` ARIMA(p, d, q) | | | | | └── q: how many past ERRORS influence today | └───── d: how many times to DIFFERENCE the data └──────── p: how many past VALUES influence today ``` ``` r # Fit a specific ARIMA model arima_model <- arima(my_timeseries, order = c(1, 1, 1)) # ARIMA(1,1,1) # Let R automatically choose the best ARIMA model library(forecast) auto_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries) summary(auto_model) forecast(auto_model, h = 12) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## How to identify p and q: ACF and PACF The **ACF** (AutoCorrelation Function) and **PACF** (Partial AutoCorrelation Function) are visual tools that help you choose p and q. ### ACF — AutoCorrelation Function Shows the correlation between the series and its own past values at each lag. **Reading the ACF plot:** - Each bar shows correlation at that lag - Dashed lines = significance boundaries (95% confidence) - Bars beyond the dashed lines = *significant* correlation at that lag ### PACF — Partial AutoCorrelation Function Shows the correlation with a specific lag *after removing the influence of all shorter lags*. ### The identification rules (the patterns to memorise) | Pattern | Meaning | Model | |---------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------| | ACF: slow decay; PACF: cuts off after lag p | Autoregressive | AR(p) | | ACF: cuts off after lag q; PACF: slow decay | Moving average | MA(q) | | Both decay slowly | Mixed | ARMA(p,q) | ``` r # Plot ACF and PACF acf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30) # AutoCorrelation Function pacf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30) # Partial AutoCorrelation Function ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** What is the difference between ACF and PACF? If the > PACF cuts off sharply after lag 2 and the ACF decays slowly, what > model would you suggest? ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Seasonal ARIMA — SARIMA ## Extending ARIMA to handle seasonality When your data has both regular autocorrelation AND seasonal patterns, you need **SARIMA**: $$\text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_m$$ The lowercase letters (p, d, q) handle the regular (non-seasonal) part — exactly as in ARIMA. The uppercase letters (P, D, Q) handle the **seasonal** part, repeating at period **m** (e.g., m = 12 for monthly data with annual seasonality). | Parameter | What it does | |-----------|-----------------------------------------------------------| | P | Seasonal AR — correlation with observations m periods ago | | D | Seasonal differencing — remove seasonal trend | | Q | Seasonal MA — correlation with seasonal errors | | m | Season length (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly) | ``` r # Automatic SARIMA selection library(forecast) sarima_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries, seasonal = TRUE) summary(sarima_model) # Manual SARIMA specification sarima_manual <- arima(my_timeseries, order = c(1,1,1), # non-seasonal part seasonal = list(order = c(1,1,1), period = 12)) # seasonal part ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Residual Diagnostics — Is Your Model Good? ## Why check residuals? After fitting any model, you must check the residuals (the leftovers). If the model has done its job, the residuals should contain **no pattern** — they should look like random white noise. Think of it like this: after a good model has explained everything it can, the only thing left should be unexplainable randomness. If you can still see a pattern in the residuals, your model is missing something. ## The four residual checks ``` r # Plot residuals in four ways par(mfrow = c(2,2)) # 1. Histogram - should look like a bell curve (normal distribution) hist(residuals(my_model)) # 2. Boxplot - should be symmetrical around zero boxplot(residuals(my_model)) # 3. Q-Q Plot - points should follow the diagonal line qqnorm(residuals(my_model)) qqline(residuals(my_model), col = "red") # 4. Residuals over time - should look random, no pattern plot(residuals(my_model)) abline(h = 0, col = "red") ``` ### What you're looking for in each plot | Plot | Good sign | Bad sign | |-----------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------| | Histogram | Symmetric bell shape | Skewed, heavy tails | | Boxplot | Symmetric, median near zero | Asymmetric, many outliers | | Q-Q Plot | Points on the diagonal line | Points curve away from line | | Time plot | Random scatter, no pattern | Visible trend or waves | ## The Ljung-Box Test A formal statistical test for whether residuals are truly random: ``` r Box.test(residuals(my_model), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box") # p-value > 0.05 → residuals are random (good!) # p-value < 0.05 → residuals still have structure (bad — model needs improvement) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You fit an ARIMA model and plot the residuals. The > Q-Q plot shows points curving off the line at both ends. What does > this tell you? Is your model acceptable? ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # The Complete Time Series Workflow ## Your step-by-step process for any dataset This is the workflow you will use in your project and exams. Memorise this sequence: ``` Step 1: LOOK at the data → Plot the time series → What do you see? Trend? Seasonality? Growing variance? → Is it additive or multiplicative? Step 2: DECOMPOSE the data → Use STL decomposition → Examine each component separately → Is the seasonal component stable? Step 3: CHECK stationarity (for ARIMA) → Plot ACF of original data — does it decay slowly? (non-stationary) → Run ADF test → Apply transformations if needed (log, differencing) Step 4: FIT models → Try multiple candidate models → For classical: SES / Holt / Holt-Winters → For stochastic: ARIMA / SARIMA → Use auto.arima() as a starting point Step 5: COMPARE models → Compare AIC, BIC, RMSE, MAE → Lower = better Step 6: DIAGNOSE residuals → Check histogram, Q-Q plot, time plot → Run Ljung-Box test → If residuals have pattern → go back to Step 4 Step 7: FORECAST → Generate forecast with confidence intervals → Plot and interpret the results ``` ## Quick R workflow template ``` r # ---- Step 1: Load and plot ---- library(forecast) library(tseries) ts_data <- ts(my_data, start = c(2000, 1), frequency = 12) plot(ts_data) # ---- Step 2: Decompose ---- stl_decomp <- stl(ts_data, s.window = "periodic") autoplot(stl_decomp) # ---- Step 3: Check stationarity ---- acf(ts_data) adf.test(ts_data) # Difference if needed ts_diff <- diff(ts_data) adf.test(ts_diff) # ---- Step 4: Fit models ---- # Classical hw_model <- hw(ts_data, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12) # ARIMA arima_auto <- auto.arima(ts_data, seasonal = TRUE) # ---- Step 5: Compare ---- accuracy(hw_model) summary(arima_auto) # check AIC # ---- Step 6: Diagnose residuals ---- checkresiduals(arima_auto) Box.test(residuals(arima_auto), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box") # ---- Step 7: Forecast ---- fc <- forecast(arima_auto, h = 12) autoplot(fc) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # How Concepts Connect Across Subjects ## Links to regression analysis (your other module) Time series and regression share the same underlying goal: **explain variation in an outcome variable**. The key differences are: | Regression | Time Series | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | Data points are independent | Data points are correlated over time | | Predict using other variables (X → Y) | Predict using own past values (Y_past → Y_future) | | Residuals should be independent | Residuals should be random (no autocorrelation) | **The bridge concept — regression with time:** Your professor mentioned that you can use regression techniques on time series data. This is called a **causal time series model** — where you use both the series' own history AND external variables (like weather, income) to explain the data. ## Links to statistics fundamentals | Concept you know | How it appears in time series | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | Normal distribution | Residuals should follow N(0, σ²) | | Hypothesis testing | ADF test for stationarity; Ljung-Box test for residuals | | Confidence intervals | Forecast intervals widen as you predict further | | Minimising sum of squared errors | How α, β, γ parameters are estimated | | Model selection (R², AIC) | AIC/BIC used to choose between models | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Quick Reference Summary ## Model selection cheat sheet ``` Look at your data: ├── No trend, no seasonality → SES / ARIMA(p,0,q) ├── Trend only → Holt's / ARIMA(p,1,q) ├── Trend + seasonality, constant swings → Holt-Winters Additive / SARIMA └── Trend + seasonality, growing swings → Holt-Winters Multiplicative (or log first) ``` ## Key terms glossary | Term | Plain English definition | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | **Time series** | Data recorded at regular intervals over time | | **Trend** | Long-term upward or downward direction | | **Seasonality** | Predictable repeating pattern within a year | | **Cyclical** | Irregular long-term waves (\>2 years) | | **Residual** | What's left after removing all patterns | | **Additive model** | Components added together; seasonal size is constant | | **Multiplicative model** | Components multiplied; seasonal size grows with level | | **Stationary** | Data with constant mean, variance, and covariance over time | | **Differencing** | Subtracting consecutive values to remove trend | | **ACF** | How correlated a series is with its own past values | | **PACF** | Direct correlation at each lag (removing shorter lag effects) | | **α (alpha)** | Smoothing parameter for the level (0–1) | | **β (beta)** | Smoothing parameter for the trend | | **γ (gamma)** | Smoothing parameter for the seasonality | | **AIC/BIC** | Model quality scores — lower means better | | **ARIMA(p,d,q)** | Autoregressive + Differencing + Moving Average model | | **SARIMA** | ARIMA extended for seasonal data | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Annotations Space *This section is intentionally left for your own notes, questions, and insights as you progress through the module.* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 1 — My notes: > *Write here after your first lecture...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 2 — My notes: > *Write here after your second lecture...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 3 onwards — Lab reflections: > *After each R lab, write one thing that surprised you and one thing > that confused you...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Questions to ask in class: > *...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Things I still don't understand: > *...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ *End of Study Guide — STAT9005 Time Series Analysis*

The "ingredients" of any time series are: ### Trend (T) — The long-term direction **What it is:** The overall movement of your data upwards or downwards over a long period. **Example:** Global average temperatures have been rising for 100 years. It can curve, slow down, or reverse. ### Seasonality (S) — The repeating pattern **What it is:** A regular, predictable pattern that repeats within a fixed period (usually within one year). **Example:** Ice cream sales are always high in summer and low in winter — every single year, without fail. Cyclical patterns take *more than 2 years* to complete and are not perfectly regular. ### Random / Residual (R) — The unexplained noise **What it is:** Everything left over after you remove trend, seasonality, and cyclical patterns. This is the unexplained, random variation. **Example:** One particular Tuesday, your coffee shop sold 300 coffees instead of the usual 200. Maybe a conference was in town. You couldn't have predicted it — that extra 100 is "random." **Key insight:** If your model is good, the residuals should look like pure random noise (no pattern left). ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The Decomposition Equation Once you accept that data is made of these components, you can write a mathematical expression for any time series observation $y_t$ at time $t$: ### Additive Model Use this when the seasonal swings stay roughly **the same size** over time. $$y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t$$ **When to use:** The ups and downs in your data look the same height throughout the series (constant variance). ### Multiplicative Model Use this when the seasonal swings get **bigger as the trend grows**. $$y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t$$ **When to use:** The wiggling in your data gets wider over time (increasing variance). **Tip:** You can convert a multiplicative model into an additive one by taking the logarithm of your data: $$\log(y_t) = \log(T_t) + \log(S_t) + \log(R_t)$$ ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You're looking at monthly electricity usage data. > In summer and winter the spikes get bigger every year. Should you use > an additive or multiplicative model? Why? > > You should use the multiplicative model because the spikes get bigger every year. Different than the additive model that stays the same size. > ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Classical Decomposition in R ## What decomposition does Decomposition is the process of separating a time series into its components so you can study each one individually. Think of it like using a prism to split white light into individual colours. In R, the main tools are: ``` r # Load the forecast library (your main toolbox for this module) library(forecast) # Basic decomposition plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "additive")) plot(decompose(my_timeseries, type = "multiplicative")) # Better decomposition: STL (Seasonal & Trend using Loess) stl_result <- stl(my_timeseries, s.window = "periodic") autoplot(stl_result) ``` ## STL Decomposition — the preferred method STL stands for **Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess** (Loess is a type of local regression). **Why STL is better than basic decomposition:** - It handles data at both ends of the series (no missing values at the start or end) - It is **robust** — extreme values (outliers) don't distort the components - You can control how flexible the trend and seasonal components are **The trade-off:** STL only works for **additive** models. For multiplicative data, take logs first, apply STL, then back-transform. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** What is the difference between `decompose()` and > `stl()` in R? Which should you prefer and why? > > For decompose() you miss values at the start or the end, outliers distort the components. stl() only works for additive models. > ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Classical Forecasting Models — Exponential Smoothing ## The core idea: recent data matters more Exponential smoothing is based on a simple but powerful idea: **the most recent observations should have more influence on your forecast than older ones.** Imagine you're estimating how long your commute will take tomorrow. Yesterday's commute matters more than the commute from 6 months ago. But you don't completely ignore the older data — you just weight it less. That's exactly what exponential smoothing does: it applies *exponentially decreasing weights* to past observations. ## The three exponential smoothing methods Think of these as tools with increasing capability: ### 1. Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) **Use when:** Data has **no trend** and **no seasonality** — just a level that fluctuates randomly. **The key parameter — α (alpha):** | Alpha value | Meaning | Practical effect | |--------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------| | α close to 1 | Short memory | Only the very last observation matters | | α close to 0 | Long memory | All past observations weighted equally | | α = 0.83 (example) | Short memory | Forecast closely tracks last value | **The formula:** $$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$$ This says: "My next forecast = (alpha × what just happened) + (1-alpha × what I previously predicted)" **In R:** ``` r library(forecast) # Method 1: specific SES function ses_model <- ses(my_timeseries, h = 5) # h = number of periods to forecast # Method 2: using the ETS family ses_model <- ets(my_timeseries, model = "ANN") # ANN = Error(Additive), No trend, No seasonality ``` **What the forecast looks like:** A flat horizontal line — because with no trend or seasonality, the best guess for all future periods is the same value. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ### 2. Holt's Method (Double Exponential Smoothing) **Use when:** Data has a **trend** but **no seasonality**. **Two parameters:** - **α (alpha):** Controls the level (same as SES) - **β (beta):** Controls how quickly the trend estimate updates **In R:** ``` r holt_model <- holt(my_timeseries, h = 5) # Damped trend (trend slows down over time — often more realistic) holt_damped <- holt(my_timeseries, damped = TRUE, h = 5) ``` **What the forecast looks like:** A sloped line following the trend direction. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ### 3. Holt-Winters Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing) **Use when:** Data has both **trend** and **seasonality** — the most complete model. **Three parameters:** - **α (alpha):** Level - **β (beta):** Trend - **γ (gamma):** Seasonality **Two versions:** - **Additive Holt-Winters:** Seasonal effect is constant in size - **Multiplicative Holt-Winters:** Seasonal effect grows with the level **In R:** ``` r # Additive hw_add <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "additive", h = 12) # Multiplicative hw_mult <- hw(my_timeseries, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12) # Let R choose automatically using ETS auto_model <- ets(my_timeseries) summary(auto_model) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The ETS Framework — the big family All exponential smoothing methods belong to the **ETS** family: **ETS = Error · Trend · Seasonality** Each letter takes a value: - **Error:** A (additive) or M (multiplicative) - **Trend:** N (none), A (additive), Ad (additive damped), M (multiplicative) - **Seasonality:** N (none), A (additive), M (multiplicative) | Model name | ETS code | Use case | |------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | Simple Exp. Smoothing | ETS(A,N,N) | No trend, no seasonality | | Holt's Method | ETS(A,A,N) | Trend, no seasonality | | Holt-Winters Additive | ETS(A,A,A) | Trend + additive seasonality | | Holt-Winters Multiplicative | ETS(A,A,M) | Trend + multiplicative seasonality | ``` r # Let R automatically find the best ETS model best_model <- ets(my_timeseries) summary(best_model) forecast(best_model, h = 12) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You have monthly data showing sales with an upward > trend and seasonal peaks at Christmas. Which ETS method would you > choose? What would α, β, and γ control? > `I would choose Holt-Winters Multiplicative because you can see an increase in sales trand, and seasonal peaks every Christmas. The Beta would be the Trend, Gamma seasonality and I am not sure about alpha.` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # How to Measure Forecast Accuracy ## Why this matters Any model can give you *a* forecast. But is it a *good* forecast? You need numbers to measure how far off your predictions are from reality. The standard approach: 1. **Split your data** into a training set (to build the model) and a test set (to check accuracy) 2. **Forecast** the test period using your model 3. **Compare** forecasts to actual values using error measures ## The main error measures Let $y_t$ = actual value and $\hat{y}_t$ = forecasted value. The error is $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$. | Measure | Formula | What it tells you | |------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum |e_t|$ | Average size of errors, ignoring direction | | **RMSE** (Root Mean Square Error) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2}$ | Penalises large errors more heavily | | **MAPE** (Mean Absolute % Error) | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \left|\frac{e_t}{y_t}\right|$ | Error as a percentage (good for comparison) | | **AIC** (Akaike Information Criterion) | — | Penalises model complexity; **lower = better** | | **BIC** (Bayesian Information Criterion) | — | Similar to AIC, stronger complexity penalty | **The golden rule for model selection:** When comparing two models, the one with the **lower AIC/BIC and lower RMSE/MAE** is preferred. ``` r # Get accuracy metrics in R accuracy(forecast_result, test_data) # Compare AIC between two models summary(model1)$aic summary(model2)$aic ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You compare two models. Model A has AIC = 1200 and > RMSE = 45. Model B has AIC = 1180 and RMSE = 52. Which would you > choose and why? (Hint: there's no single right answer — discuss the > trade-off.) ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # ARIMA Models — The Statistical Engine ## The shift in thinking Everything up to now (exponential smoothing) was about finding patterns and extrapolating them. ARIMA is different — it's based on **statistical theory** about how data relates to its own past values. The key concept: **autocorrelation** — the idea that today's value is correlated with yesterday's value, last week's value, etc. Think of it like your mood. If you're happy today, you're probably somewhat happy tomorrow. Your mood is correlated with your own past mood. Data often behaves the same way. ## Stationarity — the crucial requirement Before fitting an ARIMA model, your data must be **stationary**. This is perhaps the most important concept in the second half of the module. ### What does stationary mean? A time series is stationary if it has **three properties that don't change over time:** 1. **Constant mean** — the average doesn't drift up or down 2. **Constant variance** — the spread of the data stays roughly the same 3. **Constant covariance** — if observation at time 1 is correlated with observation at time 3 (lag 2), then this same correlation exists throughout the entire series **The visual test:** If you cover different parts of your time series plot with your hand and each section looks basically the same — same level, same spread — it's likely stationary. ### Why does stationarity matter? ARIMA models are built on statistical equations that only work when the data's statistical properties are stable. If the mean is drifting, the model can't figure out what "normal" is. ### How to make data stationary **Problem: non-constant mean (trend)** → Solution: **Differencing** Differencing means computing the change from one period to the next: $$\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$$ If differencing once doesn't work, you can do it twice (second-order differencing). The number of times you difference is the **d** in ARIMA(p, d, q). **Problem: non-constant variance** → Solution: **Log transformation** Taking the log of your data compresses large values and stabilises variance. This is the same reason multiplicative seasonal data responds to log transformation. ``` r # Visual check: does the data look stationary? plot(my_timeseries) # Differencing in R diff_series <- diff(my_timeseries) # First difference diff2_series <- diff(my_timeseries, differences = 2) # Second difference # Formal statistical test for stationarity (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) library(tseries) adf.test(my_timeseries) # p-value < 0.05 → stationary # p-value > 0.05 → NOT stationary, need differencing ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## The ARIMA building blocks **ARIMA(p, d, q)** is made of three parts: ### AR(p) — AutoRegressive **The idea:** Today's value depends on its own past values. $$y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \ldots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$ **Analogy:** "My commute today (y_t) is partly explained by my commute yesterday (y\_{t-1}) and the day before (y\_{t-2})." The **p** tells you how many past values to include. ### MA(q) — Moving Average **The idea:** Today's value depends on past *forecast errors* (not past values themselves). $$y_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \ldots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$ **Analogy:** "When I was late yesterday, I leave earlier today to compensate — I'm reacting to past *errors* in my schedule." The **q** tells you how many past errors to include. ### I(d) — Integrated **The idea:** The d stands for how many times you need to *difference* the data to make it stationary. - d = 0: already stationary - d = 1: one differencing needed (most common) - d = 2: two differences needed (rare) ### Putting it together: ARIMA(p, d, q) ``` ARIMA(p, d, q) | | | | | └── q: how many past ERRORS influence today | └───── d: how many times to DIFFERENCE the data └──────── p: how many past VALUES influence today ``` ``` r # Fit a specific ARIMA model arima_model <- arima(my_timeseries, order = c(1, 1, 1)) # ARIMA(1,1,1) # Let R automatically choose the best ARIMA model library(forecast) auto_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries) summary(auto_model) forecast(auto_model, h = 12) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## How to identify p and q: ACF and PACF The **ACF** (AutoCorrelation Function) and **PACF** (Partial AutoCorrelation Function) are visual tools that help you choose p and q. ### ACF — AutoCorrelation Function Shows the correlation between the series and its own past values at each lag. **Reading the ACF plot:** - Each bar shows correlation at that lag - Dashed lines = significance boundaries (95% confidence) - Bars beyond the dashed lines = *significant* correlation at that lag ### PACF — Partial AutoCorrelation Function Shows the correlation with a specific lag *after removing the influence of all shorter lags*. ### The identification rules (the patterns to memorise) | Pattern | Meaning | Model | |---------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------| | ACF: slow decay; PACF: cuts off after lag p | Autoregressive | AR(p) | | ACF: cuts off after lag q; PACF: slow decay | Moving average | MA(q) | | Both decay slowly | Mixed | ARMA(p,q) | ``` r # Plot ACF and PACF acf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30) # AutoCorrelation Function pacf(my_stationary_series, lag.max = 30) # Partial AutoCorrelation Function ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** What is the difference between ACF and PACF? If the > PACF cuts off sharply after lag 2 and the ACF decays slowly, what > model would you suggest? ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Seasonal ARIMA — SARIMA ## Extending ARIMA to handle seasonality When your data has both regular autocorrelation AND seasonal patterns, you need **SARIMA**: $$\text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_m$$ The lowercase letters (p, d, q) handle the regular (non-seasonal) part — exactly as in ARIMA. The uppercase letters (P, D, Q) handle the **seasonal** part, repeating at period **m** (e.g., m = 12 for monthly data with annual seasonality). | Parameter | What it does | |-----------|-----------------------------------------------------------| | P | Seasonal AR — correlation with observations m periods ago | | D | Seasonal differencing — remove seasonal trend | | Q | Seasonal MA — correlation with seasonal errors | | m | Season length (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly) | ``` r # Automatic SARIMA selection library(forecast) sarima_model <- auto.arima(my_timeseries, seasonal = TRUE) summary(sarima_model) # Manual SARIMA specification sarima_manual <- arima(my_timeseries, order = c(1,1,1), # non-seasonal part seasonal = list(order = c(1,1,1), period = 12)) # seasonal part ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Residual Diagnostics — Is Your Model Good? ## Why check residuals? After fitting any model, you must check the residuals (the leftovers). If the model has done its job, the residuals should contain **no pattern** — they should look like random white noise. Think of it like this: after a good model has explained everything it can, the only thing left should be unexplainable randomness. If you can still see a pattern in the residuals, your model is missing something. ## The four residual checks ``` r # Plot residuals in four ways par(mfrow = c(2,2)) # 1. Histogram - should look like a bell curve (normal distribution) hist(residuals(my_model)) # 2. Boxplot - should be symmetrical around zero boxplot(residuals(my_model)) # 3. Q-Q Plot - points should follow the diagonal line qqnorm(residuals(my_model)) qqline(residuals(my_model), col = "red") # 4. Residuals over time - should look random, no pattern plot(residuals(my_model)) abline(h = 0, col = "red") ``` ### What you're looking for in each plot | Plot | Good sign | Bad sign | |-----------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------| | Histogram | Symmetric bell shape | Skewed, heavy tails | | Boxplot | Symmetric, median near zero | Asymmetric, many outliers | | Q-Q Plot | Points on the diagonal line | Points curve away from line | | Time plot | Random scatter, no pattern | Visible trend or waves | ## The Ljung-Box Test A formal statistical test for whether residuals are truly random: ``` r Box.test(residuals(my_model), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box") # p-value > 0.05 → residuals are random (good!) # p-value < 0.05 → residuals still have structure (bad — model needs improvement) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ > ✏️ **Self-check:** You fit an ARIMA model and plot the residuals. The > Q-Q plot shows points curving off the line at both ends. What does > this tell you? Is your model acceptable? ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # The Complete Time Series Workflow ## Your step-by-step process for any dataset This is the workflow you will use in your project and exams. Memorise this sequence: ``` Step 1: LOOK at the data → Plot the time series → What do you see? Trend? Seasonality? Growing variance? → Is it additive or multiplicative? Step 2: DECOMPOSE the data → Use STL decomposition → Examine each component separately → Is the seasonal component stable? Step 3: CHECK stationarity (for ARIMA) → Plot ACF of original data — does it decay slowly? (non-stationary) → Run ADF test → Apply transformations if needed (log, differencing) Step 4: FIT models → Try multiple candidate models → For classical: SES / Holt / Holt-Winters → For stochastic: ARIMA / SARIMA → Use auto.arima() as a starting point Step 5: COMPARE models → Compare AIC, BIC, RMSE, MAE → Lower = better Step 6: DIAGNOSE residuals → Check histogram, Q-Q plot, time plot → Run Ljung-Box test → If residuals have pattern → go back to Step 4 Step 7: FORECAST → Generate forecast with confidence intervals → Plot and interpret the results ``` ## Quick R workflow template ``` r # ---- Step 1: Load and plot ---- library(forecast) library(tseries) ts_data <- ts(my_data, start = c(2000, 1), frequency = 12) plot(ts_data) # ---- Step 2: Decompose ---- stl_decomp <- stl(ts_data, s.window = "periodic") autoplot(stl_decomp) # ---- Step 3: Check stationarity ---- acf(ts_data) adf.test(ts_data) # Difference if needed ts_diff <- diff(ts_data) adf.test(ts_diff) # ---- Step 4: Fit models ---- # Classical hw_model <- hw(ts_data, seasonal = "multiplicative", h = 12) # ARIMA arima_auto <- auto.arima(ts_data, seasonal = TRUE) # ---- Step 5: Compare ---- accuracy(hw_model) summary(arima_auto) # check AIC # ---- Step 6: Diagnose residuals ---- checkresiduals(arima_auto) Box.test(residuals(arima_auto), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box") # ---- Step 7: Forecast ---- fc <- forecast(arima_auto, h = 12) autoplot(fc) ``` ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # How Concepts Connect Across Subjects ## Links to regression analysis (your other module) Time series and regression share the same underlying goal: **explain variation in an outcome variable**. The key differences are: | Regression | Time Series | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | Data points are independent | Data points are correlated over time | | Predict using other variables (X → Y) | Predict using own past values (Y_past → Y_future) | | Residuals should be independent | Residuals should be random (no autocorrelation) | **The bridge concept — regression with time:** Your professor mentioned that you can use regression techniques on time series data. This is called a **causal time series model** — where you use both the series' own history AND external variables (like weather, income) to explain the data. ## Links to statistics fundamentals | Concept you know | How it appears in time series | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | Normal distribution | Residuals should follow N(0, σ²) | | Hypothesis testing | ADF test for stationarity; Ljung-Box test for residuals | | Confidence intervals | Forecast intervals widen as you predict further | | Minimising sum of squared errors | How α, β, γ parameters are estimated | | Model selection (R², AIC) | AIC/BIC used to choose between models | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Quick Reference Summary ## Model selection cheat sheet ``` Look at your data: ├── No trend, no seasonality → SES / ARIMA(p,0,q) ├── Trend only → Holt's / ARIMA(p,1,q) ├── Trend + seasonality, constant swings → Holt-Winters Additive / SARIMA └── Trend + seasonality, growing swings → Holt-Winters Multiplicative (or log first) ``` ## Key terms glossary | Term | Plain English definition | |------------------------------------|------------------------------------| | **Time series** | Data recorded at regular intervals over time | | **Trend** | Long-term upward or downward direction | | **Seasonality** | Predictable repeating pattern within a year | | **Cyclical** | Irregular long-term waves (\>2 years) | | **Residual** | What's left after removing all patterns | | **Additive model** | Components added together; seasonal size is constant | | **Multiplicative model** | Components multiplied; seasonal size grows with level | | **Stationary** | Data with constant mean, variance, and covariance over time | | **Differencing** | Subtracting consecutive values to remove trend | | **ACF** | How correlated a series is with its own past values | | **PACF** | Direct correlation at each lag (removing shorter lag effects) | | **α (alpha)** | Smoothing parameter for the level (0–1) | | **β (beta)** | Smoothing parameter for the trend | | **γ (gamma)** | Smoothing parameter for the seasonality | | **AIC/BIC** | Model quality scores — lower means better | | **ARIMA(p,d,q)** | Autoregressive + Differencing + Moving Average model | | **SARIMA** | ARIMA extended for seasonal data | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Annotations Space *This section is intentionally left for your own notes, questions, and insights as you progress through the module.* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 1 — My notes: > *Write here after your first lecture...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 2 — My notes: > *Write here after your second lecture...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Week 3 onwards — Lab reflections: > *After each R lab, write one thing that surprised you and one thing > that confused you...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Questions to ask in class: > *...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## Things I still don't understand: > *...* ------------------------------------------------------------------------ *End of Study Guide — STAT9005 Time Series Analysis*
